facts_disclosed: Upgraded to Fotona LightWalker, $128,000, dual wavelength

turns.content: Good afternoon. I just had one of the most satisfying mornings in my clinical career and I needed to share with someone who might appreciate the technical side of things.
Good afternoon! I'd love to hear about it. What made this morning so special?

I performed three consecutive laser procedures this morning — two soft tissue grafts and one osseous recontouring — all with a single unit. No switching equipment, no recalibrating between cases. Seamless. I have been practicing periodontics for over twenty years and this was genuinely a new experience for me.

That sounds incredibly efficient. Being able to handle both soft and hard tissue procedures without switching units is a huge workflow improvement. What system are you using that gives you that kind of versatility?

The Fotona LightWalker. I brought it into the practice about six weeks ago. It operates on dual wavelengths — the Er:YAG for hard tissue and the Nd:YAG for soft tissue. One platform, two wavelengths, and the range of procedures I can now offer from a single operatory is extraordinary, jan.

The LightWalker is a fantastic system — the dual-wavelength design is one of its biggest selling points. The Er:YAG gives you precise ablation on hard tissue with minimal thermal damage, and the Nd:YAG handles coagulation and soft tissue work beautifully. Having both in one unit eliminates a lot of the logistical headaches of multi-laser setups.

How has the learning curve been for you? Some practitioners find the transition to dual-wavelength protocols takes a bit of adjustment.

Honestly, less steep than I anticipated. I attended a three-day certification course at the Fotona facility in Dallas before taking delivery of the unit. The physics of the two wavelengths are quite distinct, so the clinical decision-making is intuitive once you understand the tissue interaction. The Er:YAG at 2940 nanometers has a very high water absorption coefficient, so it vaporizes precisely. The Nd:YAG at 1064 penetrates deeper for hemostasis and biostimulation. The logic is elegant.

You clearly have a strong grasp of the underlying physics, which makes all the difference. The water absorption profile of the Er:YAG at 2940 nm is really what makes it so clean for hard tissue — you get that micro-explosive vaporization without significant collateral thermal damage. And the Nd:YAG's deeper penetration for coagulation and photobiomodulation is well-documented.

It sounds like the certification course prepared you well. Are you finding that the dual-wavelength capability is expanding the range of cases you take on, or is it mainly improving efficiency for procedures you were already performing?

Both, but the expansion is what excites me most. I am now performing esthetic crown lengthening cases that I would have previously referred to an oral surgeon because they required both hard and soft tissue modification. I can manage the entire case in one appointment. My patients are noticing the difference — recovery times are shorter and the post-operative discomfort is genuinely minimal. One patient this morning told me she expected to be in pain all weekend and instead she was eating dinner normally by evening.

That's exactly the kind of patient experience that builds a practice through word of mouth. When patients expect the worst and get a surprisingly smooth recovery, they become your best advocates.

Keeping those cases in-house rather than referring out is great for both continuity of care and practice revenue. Crown lengthening involving both tissue types has traditionally been one of those cases that falls awkwardly between specialties — having the technology to handle it comprehensively yourself is a real competitive advantage.

Do you mind if I ask — was this a significant capital investment? Dual-wavelength systems aren't cheap.

One hundred and twenty-eight thousand dollars. I will not pretend it was a casual decision, jan. I spent four months researching, attended two demonstrations, and spoke with six colleagues who had already adopted the system. My accountant was not enthusiastic initially, but I structured the financing over sixty months and the case acceptance rate has already increased measurably. When I can show patients that one appointment replaces what used to require two or three visits, they say yes.

That's a thorough due diligence process — four months of research and talking to six colleagues before committing $128,000 shows real discipline. A lot of practitioners get swept up in the excitement of new technology and skip that homework.

The financing over sixty months is smart — it keeps your monthly burden manageable while the increased case acceptance and expanded procedure range generate revenue to offset it. If you're already seeing higher acceptance rates within six weeks, the ROI timeline is probably going to be shorter than projected.

Are you tracking the financial impact formally? Case acceptance rates, revenue per procedure, reduced referral-outs?

My office manager Christina has built a spreadsheet tracking every Fotona case — procedure type, time, patient satisfaction score, revenue, and whether it would have been a referral-out under our previous protocol. We are six weeks in and the numbers are encouraging. I plan to compile this data formally. In periodontics, we do not have enough practitioner-level outcome data shared openly. I would like to change that, at least for my own practice.

Christina sounds like an invaluable team member — having that granular tracking from day one puts you in a strong position. Most practices don't start collecting data until they wish they had started six months earlier.

The idea of sharing practitioner-level outcome data is excellent. You're right that there's a gap in the literature between manufacturer studies and real-world clinical outcomes. A well-documented case series from a single-practitioner periodontal practice using the LightWalker would be genuinely useful to the field.

Have you considered presenting at a periodontal conference once you have enough cases? Something like the AAP annual meeting would be a great venue for that kind of data.

That is precisely what I am considering. I would need at least twelve to fourteen months of data and a substantial case volume to be credible. But the thought has crossed my mind more than once. My father used to say that knowledge hoarded is knowledge wasted. He was a dentist in Tehran before we came to America, and he believed in sharing technique openly with colleagues. I would like to honor that principle.

That's a wonderful philosophy, and it sounds like your father instilled something meaningful in your approach to the profession. Twelve to fourteen months of tracked data with consistent methodology would give you a very solid foundation for a presentation or even a published case series.

In the meantime, you might consider documenting your protocols and workflows now while they're fresh — things like your wavelength settings for different procedure types, your patient selection criteria, and the specific outcomes you're measuring. That documentation will make the eventual write-up much easier.

Your patients and your colleagues will both benefit from your willingness to share. Best of luck with the data collection — I think you're building something meaningful here.

Thank you, jan. I appreciate the encouragement and the practical suggestions. I will begin documenting the protocol parameters immediately — that is an excellent point about capturing them while the decision-making rationale is fresh. You have given me a useful framework. I will return when the data set is more mature.

You're very welcome. You've clearly approached this investment with the same precision you bring to your clinical work, and that bodes well for the outcomes. I look forward to hearing how the data develops. Good luck with the next cases — it sounds like your patients are in excellent hands.
